article.title: Zyphra Releases ZAYA1-8B, a 760M-Active-Parameter Reasoning MoE Pretrained Entirely on a 1,024-GPU AMD MI300X Cluster
article.summary: Zyphra's open-weight ZAYA1-8B ships under Apache 2.0 with 89.1 on AIME'26 and 71.0 on GPQA-Diamond, trained end-to-end on AMD Instinct MI300X GPUs and IBM Cloud.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Zyphra released ZAYA1-8B on May 6, 2026, an open-weight reasoning mixture-of-experts model with under one billion active parameters that the San Francisco company says was pretrained entirely on AMD hardware, [according to a press release distributed via PR Newswire](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/zyphra-releases-zaya1-8b-a-reasoning-model-trained-on-amd-and-optimized-for-maximum-intelligence-density-per-parameter-302764700.html). The Hugging Face model card lists the model at 760 million active parameters and 8.4 billion total parameters, [per the Zyphra/ZAYA1-8B model card on Hugging Face](https://huggingface.co/Zyphra/ZAYA1-8B).

## What We Know

Zyphra describes ZAYA1-8B as the product of a co-designed pretraining and reinforcement-learning stack aimed at maximizing reasoning performance per parameter. The model was trained on a "cluster of 1,024 MI300X GPUs with AMD Pensando Pollara interconnect" running on IBM Cloud infrastructure, [according to the press release](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/zyphra-releases-zaya1-8b-a-reasoning-model-trained-on-amd-and-optimized-for-maximum-intelligence-density-per-parameter-302764700.html). That makes ZAYA1-8B a reference point for AMD's claim that its Instinct accelerators can serve as a viable alternative to Nvidia GPUs for end-to-end frontier-class training, rather than only inference or fine-tuning workloads.

Zyphra highlighted three architectural changes the company says improve intelligence density. The first is Compressed Convolutional Attention (CCA), described as "a more efficient attention variant." The second is a "novel MLP-based expert router that improves routing stability" over standard linear routers used in many MoE designs. The third is "learned residual scaling, which controls residual-norm growth through depth," all [as detailed in the press release](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/zyphra-releases-zaya1-8b-a-reasoning-model-trained-on-amd-and-optimized-for-maximum-intelligence-density-per-parameter-302764700.html).

Alongside the model, Zyphra introduced a test-time compute method it calls Markovian RSA, "a novel test-time compute methodology that combines parallel trace generation with fixed-length context chunking," [per the same release](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/zyphra-releases-zaya1-8b-a-reasoning-model-trained-on-amd-and-optimized-for-maximum-intelligence-density-per-parameter-302764700.html). The method is positioned as a way to extend reasoning depth at inference time without growing the working context indefinitely.

The Hugging Face model card publishes per-benchmark scores. ZAYA1-8B reports 89.1 on AIME'26, 71.6 on HMMT Feb.'26, 71.0 on GPQA-Diamond, 85.58 on IFEval, 52.56 on IFBench, 65.8 on LiveCodeBench-v6, and 74.2 on MMLU-Pro, [as listed on the Zyphra/ZAYA1-8B Hugging Face page](https://huggingface.co/Zyphra/ZAYA1-8B). The press release frames those results as evidence that the model "matches or exceeds" larger competitors on AIME, HMMT, and LiveCodeBench while remaining competitive on GPQA-Diamond, IFEval, and IFBench, [according to the press release](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/zyphra-releases-zaya1-8b-a-reasoning-model-trained-on-amd-and-optimized-for-maximum-intelligence-density-per-parameter-302764700.html).

ZAYA1-8B is released under the Apache 2.0 license and made available "on Zyphra Cloud at cloud.zyphra.com and with the model weights available on Hugging Face," [per the press release](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/zyphra-releases-zaya1-8b-a-reasoning-model-trained-on-amd-and-optimized-for-maximum-intelligence-density-per-parameter-302764700.html). The Hugging Face model card confirms the license as apache-2.0, [per the Zyphra/ZAYA1-8B page](https://huggingface.co/Zyphra/ZAYA1-8B).

Zyphra's founder and chief executive framed the launch as a thesis statement about model efficiency. "ZAYA1-8B demonstrates what is possible when architecture, pretraining, and reinforcement learning are co-designed toward a single objective: maximizing the intelligence extracted per parameter and per FLOP," said Krithik Puthalath, Founder and CEO, [in the press release](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/zyphra-releases-zaya1-8b-a-reasoning-model-trained-on-amd-and-optimized-for-maximum-intelligence-density-per-parameter-302764700.html).

## What We Don't Know

The press release does not disclose the total number of training tokens, the duration of the training run on the 1,024-GPU cluster, or the cost of compute on IBM Cloud. The full benchmark methodology, including the temperature settings, sampling budget, and tool-use configuration used to produce the published scores, is not included in the press release or the Hugging Face card excerpt available at launch; the model card references a forthcoming technical report for those details.

Independent reproductions of the AIME'26, HMMT Feb.'26, and GPQA-Diamond scores have not yet been published, and the press release does not name third-party evaluators. Comparisons against specific peer models on each benchmark are referenced in summary form by Zyphra but the exact head-to-head numbers for each rival model are not enumerated in either of the cited sources, leaving room for the broader research community to confirm or contest the relative-performance claims once the technical report and reproductions are public.
